title: What a retrieval card is
user_phrasing: what is a retrieval card
why do we need cards
what does a card do
canonical_summary: A retrieval card is a compact, source-aware signpost inside a lane. It helps the model route, interpret, and answer a specific problem pattern.
source_priority_rule: Card schema and lane overview outrank ad-hoc examples.
answer_pattern: Define the card, explain its role, and connect it to routing and answer behavior.
exactness_guard: Do not invent schema fields or card roles not present in the project.

title: Cards are signposts, not chapters
user_phrasing: how long should a card be
should cards be detailed
should a card be long
canonical_summary: Cards should stay compact and explicit, especially for small models. A card should guide, not overwhelm.
source_priority_rule: Small-model guidance outranks general long-form writing instincts.
answer_pattern: Explain that compact cards improve retrieval clarity and reduce distraction.
exactness_guard: Do not turn card guidance into a chapter-length explanation.

title: Card fields that matter most
user_phrasing: what fields go in a card
what does a good card contain
canonical_summary: A strong card should include user phrasing, canonical summary, must-retrieve targets, source priority, do-not-confuse guidance, answer pattern, and exactness guard.
source_priority_rule: The schema and template define the required structure.
answer_pattern: List the core fields and explain why each helps retrieval.
exactness_guard: Do not omit exactness or do-not-confuse guidance when describing the ideal card.

title: Small-model-friendly card style
user_phrasing: how should cards be written for small models
what kind of cards do 0.8b models need
canonical_summary: Small models need cards that are short, direct, low-ambiguity, and heavy on routing cues.
source_priority_rule: Small-model mode guidance outranks generic writing style preferences.
answer_pattern: Explain that smaller models benefit from compact, clearly structured cards.
exactness_guard: Do not claim small models can handle deep context if the project says otherwise.

title: Card is too vague to route
user_phrasing: the card doesn't help
the card is too generic
the card could fit anything
canonical_summary: A vague card causes weak routing because the model cannot tell what problem pattern it should anchor to.
source_priority_rule: When routing is weak, use the explicit project card-design rules.
answer_pattern: Identify the vagueness, tighten the problem pattern, and add do-not-confuse guidance.
exactness_guard: Do not leave the card as an abstract note.

title: Card lacks exactness guard
user_phrasing: the card makes the model guess
it hallucinates exact names
it invents paths
canonical_summary: If a card lacks an exactness guard, the model may invent commands, paths, or identifiers.
source_priority_rule: Exactness guidance is mandatory when precise identifiers matter.
answer_pattern: Explain that exactness guards prevent unsupported claims.
exactness_guard: Do not treat exact identifiers as optional when the lane is precision-sensitive.

title: Card ignores distractor behavior
user_phrasing: the model keeps picking the wrong note
it blends unrelated sources
canonical_summary: A weak card often fails to state what not to confuse, so distractor retrieval wins.
source_priority_rule: Do-not-confuse rules outrank assumptions that the model will infer boundaries itself.
answer_pattern: Show that the fix is to make boundaries explicit.
exactness_guard: Do not assume the model will naturally ignore distractors.

title: When to split a card vs split a lane
user_phrasing: should this be a new card or a new lane
do I need a new lane for this
canonical_summary: If the issue is a repeated subpattern inside one domain, create a card. If it represents a distinct routing destination or answer style, consider a new lane.
answer_pattern: Explain the decision rule and recommend the safer smaller change when evidence is weak.
exactness_guard: Do not create a new lane just because a title sounds cool.

title: When to rewrite vs retire a card
user_phrasing: should we fix this card or delete it
this card is bad what now
canonical_summary: Rewrite a card if the pattern is real but poorly expressed. Retire it if it duplicates another card or adds confusion.
source_priority_rule: Failure analysis and coverage review drive the decision.
answer_pattern: Recommend either rewrite or retirement based on duplication and confusion risk.
exactness_guard: Do not keep a weak card just because it already exists.

title: Source priority for card writing
user_phrasing: what source wins when writing a card
how do I choose what a card should say
canonical_summary: Card writing should prioritize canonical lane files, schema definitions, and source-backed references over old notes or memory.
source_priority_rule: Canonical lane files and schema definitions outrank old archive notes.
answer_pattern: State the source order clearly before giving card-writing advice.
exactness_guard: Do not let old notes override the current schema.

title: Do not confuse a card with a document chunk
user_phrasing: is a card just a chunk
isn't this just RAG chunking
canonical_summary: A card is not the same as a generic chunk. A card is deliberately shaped with routing and answer behavior.
source_priority_rule: Atlas-specific definitions outrank generic RAG assumptions.
answer_pattern: Explain the difference between a compact signpost and a plain chunk of text.
exactness_guard: Do not reduce Atlas cards to ordinary chunking.

title: How to answer card-design questions
user_phrasing: how should the assistant respond about cards
what is the response shape for card design
canonical_summary: Responses about card design should define the role, identify the problem, point to the relevant fields, and recommend the next small fix.
source_priority_rule: Answer-pattern guidance should shape the response.
answer_pattern: Use a compact explanation plus a concrete next step.
exactness_guard: Do not answer with only abstract theory.
